text: The surgeon demonstrates the port placement for the adrenalectomy on an obese patient. A camera port is positioned pararectally, two centimeters above the umbilicus, using a twelve millimeter air seal port to maintain optimal pressure during the procedure.

The surgical team places two five-millimeter ports in the abdominal wall, aligning them to facilitate access to the adrenal gland. The surgeon positions the ports to allow optimal maneuverability, with two ports for the surgeon, one assisting port, and one for the camera holder.
The surgeon identifies the spleen and bowel, then begins a lateral colic incision to approach the kidney pedicle.

The surgeon identifies the pedicle of the renal artery and vein as key landmarks for adrenalectomy, emphasizing the importance of adequately mobilizing the colon to access these structures effectively.
The surgeon mobilizes the spleen, using a bipolar instrument from Carl Storz, visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon mobilizes the spleen by freeing it from its posterior attachments, allowing it to fall down to the right side. The patient is positioned in a complete lateral decubitus position to facilitate this maneuver.

The surgeon uses a 30-degree lens to provide a clear view of the surgical field, focusing on the mobilization of the spleen. The proximity to the bowel is noted, highlighting the risk of damage if excessive energy is applied.
The surgeon identifies the kidney and the mesocolon, discussing the plan to mobilize the entire kidney during the adrenalectomy procedure.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the upper pole of the kidney, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues. The pancreas is nearby, and care is taken to avoid injuring the pancreas wall during the preparation of the area for addressing the metastasis.

The surgeon identifies the tumor and attempts to locate the vessels to begin the section of the adrenal gland. The visual field shows the tumor and surrounding adrenal tissue, with the surgeon preparing for a radical adrenalectomy.
The surgeon identifies Gerota's fascia and searches for the pedicle, noting the potential challenge of cell spillage when dealing with metastasis.
The surgeon identifies the genital vein on the right edge of the visual field and considers following it. The adrenal gland is visible, surrounded by a distinct tissue layer.
The surgeon identifies a vessel, likely the renal vein, with a branch extending towards the adrenal mass. The camera zooms out slightly to provide a broader view of the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the adrenal vein adjacent to the tumor, noting the presence of hard tissue nearby. Care is taken to avoid cutting into the tumor while adjusting the lens orientation to maintain anatomical clarity.
The surgeon identifies a vein clearly leading to the tumor and pushes down on hard tissue, suspected to be lymph nodes, while requesting a hemolog for ligation.

The surgeon applies hemolog clips to secure the vessels, using medium-sized clips for larger vessels and smaller clips for smaller vessels. Scissors are used to cut the vessels after clipping. The surgical field includes the kidney and tumor, with the kidney surface visible.

The surgeon identifies a large vessel, uncertain if it is an adrenal vessel or an upper pole vessel to the kidney, and proceeds with further mobilization to clarify its identity. Care is taken to preserve the kidney's blood supply during this process.

The surgeon considers following the medial aspect of the surgical field, while assessing an uncertain vessel. The mobilization allows the entire tissue package to be lifted upwards, revealing minor vessels that can be cut with the current instrument.
The surgeon examines a vessel, considering it might be a lymphatic vessel, and decides to mobilize the surrounding tissue for better visualization and orientation.

The surgeon identifies the tumor and notes the importance of adequate spleen mobilization to access the area. A clear dissection plane is visible, and the tumor appears large. The camera view is adjusted for closer inspection.

The surgeon navigates through the tissue, identifying small upper pole vessels that require careful control to prevent bleeding. The use of coagulation and a smoke evacuation system is visible, aiding in maintaining a clear view of the surgical field. Lymphatic vessels are lifted for better anatomical visualization.

The surgeon identifies the whole tumor and the healthy part of the adrenal gland, distinguishing the metastasis. The patient is in a lateral position, providing access to the kidney and pedicle. The artery is visible, requiring careful handling.

The surgeon identifies the psoas muscle as a landmark, surrounded by adipose tissue, and notes its proximity to the kidney. The anatomy is clearly visible, indicating that most of the dissection work is completed.
The surgeon identifies lymph fluid and the medial vein of the adrenal gland. A Hem-o-lok clip is applied to the vein as the anatomy is defined for precise dissection.

The surgeon attempts to rotate the tumor, revealing remaining adhesions. Using a no-touch technique, the tumor is carefully circumnavigated to avoid mobilizing cells. The healthy adrenal tissue is visible, with the main mass of the tumor located on one side.

The surgeon uses a second forceps to manipulate the tissue around the kidney pedicle, employing a thirty-degree lens for better visualization, especially in obese patients. Hemolock clips are used to secure vessels during the procedure.
The surgeon removes a port, reduces the pressure, and checks the port sites for any bleeding. Suction is applied to remove lymph fluid from the surgical area.
